title: Playing for Change: Music and Musicians in the Service of Social Movements By Rob Rosenthal and Richard Flacks Paradigm Publishers. 2012. 320 pages. $23.76 paperback, $138.55 hardback
abstract: Rosenthal and Flacks's Playing for Change: Music and Musicians in the Service of Social Movements provides an astute analysis of how music in all its multifaceted forms and genres can serve an array of functions for social movements. Outside of appealing to scholars of culture and social movements, this book also speaks to activists and musicians, providing a much-needed analysis of the complicated—yet often synergistic—role that music can play in movements. In addition, even younger audiences of undergraduate students will enjoy some of the more colorful quoted passages from musicians who were interviewed for the book. Clearly, Rosenthal and Flacks's commitment to in-depth empirical research, along with providing rich theoretical insights, should place this work on the same bookshelf as other classics within the field of music and social movements (e.g., Denisoff's Sing a Song of Social Significance [1972]; Eyerman and Jamison's Music and Social Movements [1998]; Roscigno and Danaher's Voice of Southern Labor [2004]; and Roy's Reds, Whites, and Blues [2010]).
